era_context: Late 14th century: Ming Dynasty consolidates power in China; Ottoman Empire expands in the Balkans; Western Christendom fractured by the Great Schism (1378–1417); Timurid Renaissance in Persia; Joseon Dynasty in Korea; Aztec Empire in Mesoamerica.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe and Scandinavia (sparse specific records for this year)

events.title: Peasants' Revolt aftermath and legal consolidation in England
events.description: In the years following the Peasants' Revolt of 1381, English authorities consolidated legal repression against the rebels and their sympathizers. By 1382, the revolt had been thoroughly suppressed, and the crown worked to restore feudal order. The revolt's failure demonstrated the resilience of the feudal system despite significant social stress, though it also exposed the fragility of the social order and contributed to long-term changes in labor relations and peasant consciousness.
events.certainty_note: The aftermath of the Peasants' Revolt is well-documented in English chronicles and legal records.

events.title: Ming Dynasty consolidation: Zhu Yuanzhang's administrative reforms
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang) continued his administrative consolidation of the Ming Dynasty, strengthening centralized control and eliminating potential rivals. In 1382, he reorganized the provincial administration and reinforced the examination system for selecting officials, which would become a hallmark of Ming governance. These reforms aimed to prevent the fragmentation that had characterized the Yuan Dynasty and to establish a stable bureaucratic apparatus.
events.certainty_note: Ming administrative reforms are well-documented in official records, though the precise chronology of specific reforms in 1382 is less detailed than for major events.

events.title: Ottoman expansion: Murad I's continued Balkan campaigns
events.description: Ottoman Sultan Murad I continued his systematic conquest of the Balkans, consolidating control over Serbian and Bulgarian territories. The Ottoman military machine, particularly the Janissary corps, established increasing dominance in the region. These campaigns set the stage for the Battle of Kosovo (1389) and the eventual Ottoman conquest of the Balkans.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in the Balkans during the 1380s is well-attested. Specific events of 1382 are less precisely dated but fit the documented pattern of Murad I's campaigns.

events.title: Timurid Renaissance: Timur's patronage of arts and architecture
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane), the Central Asian conqueror, continued to patronize the arts and architecture in his empire. The Timurid Renaissance, which flourished under his rule and that of his successors, produced remarkable achievements in Persian literature, miniature painting, calligraphy, and architecture. By 1382, Timur's court at Samarkand had become a major center of cultural production, attracting scholars and artists from across the Islamic world.

sources.name: Persian chronicles (Yazdi, Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi)

events.certainty_note: The Timurid Renaissance is well-documented through surviving architectural monuments and later chronicles. Specific cultural achievements in 1382 are less precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The Peasants' Revolt of 1381 is sometimes romanticized as a proto-democratic uprising, but modern scholarship emphasizes its limited social base and the rapid restoration of feudal order. The revolt did not fundamentally alter the feudal system, though it did contribute to long-term changes in labor relations. Ottoman expansion in the Balkans is sometimes portrayed as inevitable, but the resistance of Serbian and Byzantine forces was more substantial than earlier historiography suggested.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1382 is moderate. English, Chinese, Ottoman, and Persian sources provide reasonable coverage of major political and military events. The year falls within a period of significant geopolitical transitions. African, American, and Oceanic histories remain severely underrepresented in surviving written records. The density of documentation is uneven across regions, with Western Europe and East Asia better represented than other areas.

graph_edges.note: The Peasants' Revolt of 1381 was followed by legal consolidation and repression in 1382.
